Using the Pythagorean Identity from Definition 8.6 with \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\) as the true parameter (which lies in \(\Theta_0\) under the null hypothesis): \[n \|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2 = n \|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2 - n \|\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2\]
Distributional Conclusion
Multiplying by \(n\), we identify the terms with Chi-square variables: \[\underbrace{D}_{\text{Statistic}} \approx \underbrace{n\|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2}_{\sim \chi^2_{p_1}} - \underbrace{n\|\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2}_{\sim \chi^2_{p_0}}\]
By Cochran’s Theorem, the squared length of the projection of a standard normal vector onto the orthogonal complement of a subspace of dimension \(p_0\) is distributed as a Chi-square with degrees of freedom equal to the codimension: \[D \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_{p_1 - p_0}\]

References: Le Cam, L. (1986). Asymptotic Methods in Statistical Decision Theory. Springer-Verlag.


Example 8.5 LRT for Normal Mean with Unknown Variance
Let \(X_1, \dots, X_n \overset{i.i.d.}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) where both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are unknown. The parameter vector is \(\theta = (\mu, \sigma^2)\). We wish to test: \[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\]

Find the Maximum Likelihood Estimators: The log-likelihood function is \(\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu)^2\).

Unrestricted MLEs (\(\hat{\theta}\)): \(\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar{X})^2\). The maximized log-likelihood is: \[\ell(\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\hat{\sigma}^2) - \frac{n}{2}\]
Restricted MLEs (\(\hat{\theta}_0\)): Under \(H_0\), \(\hat{\mu}_0 = \mu_0, \quad \hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0)^2\). The restricted maximized log-likelihood is: \[\ell(\hat{\mu}_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\hat{\sigma}_0^2) - \frac{n}{2}\]

Define the Test Statistic \(D\): The test statistic \(D\) is defined as twice the difference between the unrestricted and restricted log-likelihoods: \[D = 2 \left[ \ell(\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^2) - \ell(\hat{\mu}_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2) \right]\] Substituting the expressions above, the constant terms cancel out: \[D = 2 \left[ -\frac{n}{2}\ln(\hat{\sigma}^2) - \left( -\frac{n}{2}\ln(\hat{\sigma}_0^2) \right) \right] = n \ln \left( \frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2} \right)\]
Relate to the t-statistic: Using the sum of squares decomposition \(\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 + (\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2\), we have: \[\frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2} = 1 + \frac{(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2}\] Substituting the t-statistic \(T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}}\), where \(S^2 = \frac{n}{n-1}\hat{\sigma}^2\), we find: \[\frac{(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2} = \frac{T^2}{n-1}\] Thus, \(D\) is a monotone increasing function of \(T^2\): \[D = n \ln \left( 1 + \frac{T^2}{n-1} \right)\] Rejecting \(H_0\) for large \(D\) is equivalent to rejecting for large \(|T|\).
Asymptotic Distribution (Wilks’ Theorem): By Wilks’ Theorem, \(D \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1\). For large \(n\), using the approximation \(\ln(1+x) \approx x\): \[D = n \ln \left( 1 + \frac{T^2}{n-1} \right) \approx n \left( \frac{T^2}{n-1} \right) \approx T^2\]
As \(n \to \infty\), \(T^2 \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1\), confirming the theorem.

title: 9  Appendices
text: A Short List of Contributors to Statistical Inference Based On Likelihood

title: 9  Appendices
text:
R.A. Fisher (1922, 1925)

Affiliation: Rothamsted / University College London (UCL)
Connection: The “father” of the field; his work at UCL influenced the entire next generation, including Rao and Pearson.
Contribution: Fisher formally defined Likelihood as a function distinct from probability (\(L(\theta; x)\) vs \(f(x; \theta)\)). He introduced the Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE), derived the Fisher Information measure, and established the asymptotic efficiency of MLEs.

J. Neyman & E.S. Pearson (1928, 1933)

Affiliations: UC Berkeley (Neyman) / UCL (Pearson)
Connection: Egon Pearson was Karl Pearson’s son (Fisher’s rival). Neyman founded the Berkeley Statistics department, which later hosted Lehmann, Scheffé, and Le Cam.
Contribution: They introduced the Likelihood Ratio criterion (\(\lambda\)) as a general method for hypothesis testing. Their 1933 Lemma proved that for simple hypotheses, the Likelihood Ratio Test is the Uniformly Most Powerful (UMP) test, establishing the optimality of likelihood-based methods.

S.S. Wilks (1938)

Affiliation: Princeton University
Connection: A student of Henry Rietz, Wilks spent time at UCL with Pearson and Wishart before establishing the Princeton program.
Contribution: Wilks derived the asymptotic distribution of the Likelihood Ratio statistic for composite hypotheses. He proved that \(-2 \ln \Lambda\) converges to a Chi-square distribution with degrees of freedom equal to the difference in the number of free parameters.

M.S. Bartlett (1937)

Affiliation: UCL / University of Manchester
Connection: A student of Fisher and colleague of Pearson at UCL; famously debated with Fisher on conditional inference.
Contribution: Bartlett improved the accuracy of the Likelihood Ratio Test for small samples. He introduced the Bartlett Correction, a scaling factor for the test statistic that aligns its expected value with that of the limiting Chi-square distribution.

A. Wald (1943)

Affiliation: Columbia University
Connection: A student of Karl Menger in Vienna; at Columbia, he worked with Wolfowitz and influenced the decision-theoretic approach adopted by Stein and Karlin.
Contribution: Wald developed the Wald Test, which tests hypotheses based on the distance between the unrestricted MLE and the hypothesized value. He demonstrated the asymptotic equivalence of the Likelihood Ratio, Wald, and Score tests for standard models.

H. Cramér (1946)

Affiliation: Stockholm University
Connection: Hosted Rao in Stockholm; his textbook synthesized the British (Fisher/Pearson) and American (Wilks/Wald) schools into one rigorous framework.
Contribution: In his influential text Mathematical Methods of Statistics, Cramér provided rigorous proofs for the consistency and asymptotic normality of the MLE. Independently of Rao, he established the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound.

C.R. Rao (1948)

Affiliation: Indian Statistical Institute (ISI)
Connection: A PhD student of Fisher at Cambridge; he unified the testing theories of Fisher, Neyman, and Wald.
Contribution: Rao introduced the Score Test (or Lagrange Multiplier Test), which allows for hypothesis testing using only the restricted MLE (under the null hypothesis). He also independently derived the lower bound for estimator variance.

E.L. Lehmann & H. Scheffé (1950, 1955)

Affiliation: UC Berkeley
Connection: Lehmann was Neyman’s student at Berkeley; Scheffé was a colleague. They solidified the “Berkeley School” of optimality.
Contribution: Building on the concept of Sufficiency, they established the Lehmann-Scheffé Theorem. This result connects complete sufficient statistics to Uniformly Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimators (UMVUE).

S. Karlin & H. Rubin (1956)

Affiliation: Stanford University
Connection: Part of the “Stanford School” (along with Stein) that focused on decision theory, heavily influenced by Wald’s earlier work.
Contribution: They formalized the property of Monotone Likelihood Ratio (MLR) for families of distributions. The Karlin-Rubin Theorem extended the Neyman-Pearson Lemma to one-sided composite hypotheses.

W. James & C. Stein (1961)

Affiliation: Stanford University
Connection: Stein was a student of Wald at Columbia before moving to Stanford.
Contribution: They discovered Stein’s Paradox, showing that the MLE (sample mean) is inadmissible for estimating the mean of a multivariate Normal distribution (\(p \ge 3\)). They introduced the James-Stein Estimator, a shrinkage estimator that dominates the MLE.

L. Le Cam (1960s)

Affiliation: UC Berkeley
Connection: A student of Neyman at Berkeley; he took the asymptotic torch from Wald and Wilks and placed it on a rigorous topological foundation.
Contribution: Le Cam modernized asymptotic theory by introducing Local Asymptotic Normality (LAN). He showed that under general conditions, the likelihood ratio of a complex experiment behaves asymptotically like that of a Normal shift experiment.

title: 8  Likelihood-based Hypothesis Testing
section: 8.6 Rao’s Score Theorem for Testing Restricted Models
text:
8.6 Rao’s Score Theorem for Testing Restricted Models
The asymptotic distribution of the Score Test statistic relies on the behavior of the gradient of the log-likelihood (the Score vector) evaluated at the restricted Maximum Likelihood Estimator. This theorem provides a powerful method for hypothesis testing that only requires fitting the model under the null hypothesis.

Theorem 8.5 (Asymptotic Distribution of the Score Statistic) Let \(\mathbf{Y} = (Y_1, \dots, Y_n)\) be an i.i.d. sample from a regular family of distributions with parameters \(\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{p}\). Partition the parameter vector as \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^T, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1^T)^T\), where \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\) is the \(q\)-dimensional parameter of interest and \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\) is the \((p-q)\)-dimensional nuisance parameter. Consider the test: \[H_0: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^* \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 \neq \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*\]
Let \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1)\) be the restricted MLE under \(H_0\). Let \(\mathbf{U}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) be the total Score vector partitioned as \(\mathbf{U} = [\mathbf{U}_0^T, \mathbf{U}_1^T]^T\), and let \(\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) be the expected Fisher Information matrix with \(\mathcal{I}^{00}\) representing the top-left block of its inverse \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\).
The Score statistic is \(S = \mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)^T \mathcal{I}^{00}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) \mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)\). Under \(H_0\), as \(n \to \infty\): \[S \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\] where \(q\) is the dimension of the parameter of interest \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\).


Proof. Algebraic Proof via Orthogonal Projection of the Score
We rely on the first-order Taylor expansion to observe how estimating the nuisance parameter \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\) impacts the sampling distribution of the score vector for the parameter of interest \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\).

Partitioning the Information Matrix The expected Fisher Information matrix and its inverse are partitioned conformably with \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^T, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1^T)^T\): \[\mathcal{I} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathcal{I}_{00} & \mathcal{I}_{01} \\ \mathcal{I}_{10} & \mathcal{I}_{11} \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathcal{I}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathcal{I}^{00} & \mathcal{I}^{01} \\ \mathcal{I}^{10} & \mathcal{I}^{11} \end{pmatrix}\]
Estimation Error of the Nuisance Parameter Let \(\mathbf{U}_0\) and \(\mathbf{U}_1\) denote the unobservable true score vectors evaluated at the true parameters \((\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1)\). By definition, the restricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1\) forces the nuisance score to zero: \(\mathbf{U}_1(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1) = \mathbf{0}\).
Taking a first-order Taylor expansion of \(\mathbf{U}_1(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1)\) around the true parameter \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\): \[\mathbf{0} \approx \mathbf{U}_1 - \mathcal{I}_{11} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_1)\] Rearranging gives the asymptotic estimation error: \[(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_1) \approx \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \mathbf{U}_1\]
The Effective Score (Orthogonal Projection) Next, we expand the evaluated score of interest \(\mathbf{U}_0(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1)\) around the true parameter \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\): \[\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) \approx \mathbf{U}_0 - \mathcal{I}_{01} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_1)\]
Substituting the estimation error from Step 2: \[\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) \approx \mathbf{U}_0 - \mathcal{I}_{01} \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \mathbf{U}_1\]
This quantity is the Effective Score. Geometrically, it represents the residual of \(\mathbf{U}_0\) after projecting it orthogonally onto the nuisance score \(\mathbf{U}_1\).
Asymptotic Variance of the Effective Score We verify independence by checking the covariance between the effective score and the nuisance score: \[\text{Cov}(\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0), \mathbf{U}_1) \approx \text{Cov}(\mathbf{U}_0, \mathbf{U}_1) - \mathcal{I}_{01} \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \text{Var}(\mathbf{U}_1) = \mathcal{I}_{01} - \mathcal{I}_{01} \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \mathcal{I}_{11} = \mathbf{0}\]
Because they are jointly normal with zero covariance, they are independent. The variance of this orthogonalized score is the Schur complement of \(\mathcal{I}_{11}\): \[\text{Var}(\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)) = \mathcal{I}_{00} - \mathcal{I}_{01} \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \mathcal{I}_{10}\] By the block matrix inversion theorem, this Schur complement is exactly the inverse of the top-left block of \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\): \[\text{Var}(\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)) = (\mathcal{I}^{00})^{-1}\]
Distributional Conclusion Because the evaluated score \(\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)\) is asymptotically normal with mean zero and variance \((\mathcal{I}^{00})^{-1}\), standardizing it by its variance creates a quadratic form that follows a Chi-square distribution: \[S = \mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)^T \mathcal{I}^{00} \mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\]



Example 8.6 Score Test for Normal Mean with Unknown Variance
Let \(X_1, \dots, X_n \overset{i.i.d.}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) where both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are unknown. The parameter vector is \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mu, \sigma^2)^T\). We wish to test: \[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\]

Find the Restricted MLEs: Under \(H_0\), we fix \(\mu = \mu_0\). Maximizing the restricted log-likelihood with respect to the nuisance parameter \(\sigma^2\) yields: \[\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0)^2\] The restricted parameter vector is \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 = (\mu_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2)^T\).
Evaluate the Full Score Vector: The full log-likelihood function for the normal sample is: \[\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu)^2\]
The total Score vector \(\mathbf{U}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) is the gradient with respect to both parameters: \[\mathbf{U}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{pmatrix} U_\mu(\mu, \sigma^2) \\ U_{\sigma^2}(\mu, \sigma^2) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu)}{\sigma^2} \\ -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu)^2 \end{pmatrix}\]
Now we evaluate this full vector at the restricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 = (\mu_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2)^T\): \[\mathbf{U}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \\ -\frac{n}{2\hat{\sigma}_0^2} + \frac{1}{2\hat{\sigma}_0^4}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0)^2 \end{pmatrix}\]
Because \(\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0)^2\), the second component perfectly cancels out. By definition, the score for the nuisance parameter evaluated at the restricted MLE is zero: \[\mathbf{U}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\]
Determine the Fisher Information: The expected Fisher Information matrix for a Normal distribution is diagonal because the mean and variance are orthogonal parameters: \[\mathcal{I}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n}{\sigma^2} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{n}{2\sigma^4} \end{pmatrix}\] Because the matrix is diagonal, the top-left block of the inverse \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\) is simply the reciprocal of the top-left block of \(\mathcal{I}\). Evaluated at \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0\): \[\mathcal{I}^{\mu\mu}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{n}\]
Compute the Score Statistic \(S\): Constructing the quadratic form for the parameter of interest: \[S = U_\mu(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)^T \mathcal{I}^{\mu\mu}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) U_\mu(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)\] \[S = \left( \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \right) \left( \frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{n} \right) \left( \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \right) = \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}\]
Asymptotic Distribution: By the Score Theorem, since we are testing \(q=1\) parameter restriction, the statistic follows a \(\chi^2_1\) distribution: \[S = \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1\] This is highly analogous to the squared t-statistic, but specifically uses the variance estimated under the null hypothesis constraint.

title: 8  Likelihood-based Hypothesis Testing
section: 8.7 Wald’s Theorem for Testing Parameter Restrictions
text:
8.7 Wald’s Theorem for Testing Parameter Restrictions
The Wald Test evaluates the distance between the unrestricted Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) and the null hypothesis value, standardized by the curvature of the log-likelihood at the unrestricted estimate. Unlike the Score test, the Wald test requires estimating the full, unrestricted model.

Theorem 8.6 (Asymptotic Distribution of the Wald Statistic) Let \(\mathbf{Y} = (Y_1, \dots, Y_n)\) be an i.i.d. sample from a regular family of distributions with parameters \(\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{p}\). Partition the parameter vector as \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^T, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1^T)^T\), where \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\) is the \(q\)-dimensional parameter of interest and \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\) is the \((p-q)\)-dimensional nuisance parameter. Consider the test: \[H_0: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^* \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 \neq \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*\]
Let \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0^T, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1^T)^T\) be the unrestricted MLE under \(H_1\). Let \(\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) be the expected Fisher Information matrix, and let \(\mathcal{I}^{00}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) denote the top-left \(q \times q\) block of its inverse \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\).
The Wald statistic is defined as the quadratic form: \[W = (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*)^T [\mathcal{I}^{00}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})]^{-1} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*)\]
Under standard regularity conditions, if \(H_0\) is true, as \(n \to \infty\): \[W \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\] where \(q\) is the dimension of the parameter of interest \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\).


Proof. Algebraic Proof via Asymptotic Normality of the MLE
The derivation of the Wald statistic relies directly on the asymptotic sampling distribution of the Maximum Likelihood Estimator.

Asymptotic Normality of the Full MLE By standard large-sample theory, the unrestricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\) is consistent and asymptotically normal. Its asymptotic variance-covariance matrix is the inverse of the Fisher Information matrix evaluated at the true parameter \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\): \[\sqrt{n}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} - \boldsymbol{\theta}) \xrightarrow{d} N(\mathbf{0}, [n^{-1}\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta})]^{-1})\] Equivalently, for the unscaled estimator: \[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \overset{a}{\sim} N(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathcal{I}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta}))\]
Marginal Distribution of the Parameter of Interest We partition the inverse Fisher Information matrix \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\) conformably with \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^T, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1^T)^T\). The marginal asymptotic distribution of the sub-vector \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0\) depends only on the corresponding top-left block of the inverse matrix, \(\mathcal{I}^{00}\): \[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 \overset{a}{\sim} N(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0, \mathcal{I}^{00}(\boldsymbol{\theta}))\]
Under the null hypothesis \(H_0: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*\), this becomes: \[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 \overset{a}{\sim} N(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \mathcal{I}^{00}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1))\]
Constructing the Quadratic Form
To create a test statistic, we measure the squared distance from the estimate \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0\) to the hypothesized value \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*\), standardized by its variance. If a random vector \(\mathbf{Z} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{\Sigma})\), then the quadratic form \((\mathbf{Z} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{Z} - \boldsymbol{\mu})\) follows a \(\chi^2\) distribution with degrees of freedom equal to the dimension of \(\mathbf{Z}\).
Applying this to our marginal distribution: \[(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*)^T [\mathcal{I}^{00}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1)]^{-1} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*) \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\]
Substituting the Consistent Estimator In practice, the true parameters in the variance matrix are unknown. Because the Fisher Information matrix is a continuous function of the parameters, and \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\) is a consistent estimator of \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\), we can invoke Slutsky’s Theorem. We substitute the unknown true information with the expected information evaluated at the unrestricted MLE, \(\mathcal{I}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})\), without changing the asymptotic distribution: \[W = (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*)^T [\mathcal{I}^{00}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})]^{-1} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*) \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\]



Example 8.7 Wald Test for Normal Mean with Unknown Variance
Let \(X_1, \dots, X_n \overset{i.i.d.}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) where both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are unknown. The parameter vector is \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mu, \sigma^2)^T\). We wish to test: \[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\]

Find the Unrestricted MLEs: Maximizing the full log-likelihood with respect to both parameters yields the unrestricted estimators: \[\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar{X})^2\] Our unrestricted parameter estimate is \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^2)^T\).
Determine the Fisher Information at the MLE: The expected Fisher Information matrix for a Normal distribution is diagonal: \[\mathcal{I}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n}{\sigma^2} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{n}{2\sigma^4} \end{pmatrix}\] Because the matrix is diagonal, the top-left block of the inverse \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\) (the asymptotic variance of \(\hat{\mu}\)) is simply the reciprocal of the top-left block of \(\mathcal{I}\). Evaluated at the unrestricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\): \[\mathcal{I}^{\mu\mu}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) = \frac{\hat{\sigma}^2}{n}\]
Compute the Wald Statistic \(W\): We construct the quadratic form using the parameter of interest (\(\mu\)), its null value (\(\mu_0\)), and its estimated variance: \[W = (\hat{\mu} - \mu_0)^T [\mathcal{I}^{\mu\mu}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})]^{-1} (\hat{\mu} - \mu_0)\] Substituting our values: \[W = (\bar{X} - \mu_0) \left( \frac{n}{\hat{\sigma}^2} \right) (\bar{X} - \mu_0) = \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2}\]
Contrast with the Score Statistic: By Wald’s Theorem, this statistic follows a \(\chi^2_1\) distribution asymptotically. Note the critical difference from the Score test derived previously. The Wald statistic uses the unrestricted variance estimator \(\hat{\sigma}^2\), whereas the Score statistic used the restricted variance estimator \(\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (X_i - \mu_0)^2\). Because \(\hat{\sigma}_0^2 \ge \hat{\sigma}^2\), it guarantees that mathematically \(W \ge S\) for this model.

title: 8  Likelihood-based Hypothesis Testing
text:
8.5 LRT, Score, and Wald’s Tests

8.5.1 Likelihood Ratio Tests
The Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) provides a unified approach for constructing hypothesis tests for parametric models. Let \(\mathbf{Y} = (Y_1, \dots, Y_n)\) be a random sample with joint probability density function \(L(\boldsymbol{\theta}; \mathbf{Y})\), where \(\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta\). We consider the general hypothesis testing problem: \[H_0: \boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta \setminus \Theta_0\]
The Likelihood Ratio Statistic is defined as the ratio of the maximum likelihood achievable under the null hypothesis to the maximum likelihood achievable under the full parameter space:
\[\Lambda(\mathbf{Y}) = \frac{\sup_{\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta_0} L(\boldsymbol{\theta}; \mathbf{Y})}{\sup_{\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta} L(\boldsymbol{\theta}; \mathbf{Y})} = \frac{L(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)})}{L(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)})}\]
where \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\) is the restricted MLE and \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}\) is the unrestricted (global) MLE. By definition, the likelihood ratio is bounded by \(0 \le \Lambda(\mathbf{Y}) \le 1\). The Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT) is defined by the decision function:
\[
\phi(\mathbf{Y}) = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \Lambda(\mathbf{Y}) \le c \quad (\text{Reject } H_0) \\
0 & \text{if } \Lambda(\mathbf{Y}) > c \quad (\text{Fail to reject } H_0)
\end{cases}
\]
where \(c \in (0, 1)\) is a critical value determined by the significance level \(\alpha\). This rejection region \(\{\mathbf{Y} : \Lambda(\mathbf{Y}) \le c\}\) corresponds to cases where the restricted likelihood (under \(H_0\)) is significantly smaller than the unrestricted likelihood.


8.5.2 Geometry of Likelihood
Before deriving the asymptotic distribution of the LRT, we establish the geometric framework. We define the inner product on the parameter space induced by the Fisher Information \(\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0)\) as \(\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle_{\mathcal{I}} = \mathbf{u}^T \mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0) \mathbf{v}\).

Definition 8.6 (Definition: Orthogonal Projection under Fisher Information) Let \(\Theta_0\) be a linear subspace of the parameter space. The orthogonal projection of a point \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}\) onto \(\Theta_0\) with respect to the inner product \(\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_{\mathcal{I}}\) is the unique vector \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} \in \Theta_0\) such that the residual is orthogonal to the subspace: \[ \langle \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}, \mathbf{v} \rangle_{\mathcal{I}} = 0 \quad \text{for all } \mathbf{v} \in \Theta_0 \]
Consequence (Pythagorean Identity): As a direct consequence of this definition, for any \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0 \in \Theta_0\), the vector \((\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0)\) lies in \(\Theta_0\) and is therefore orthogonal to the residual \((\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)})\). This implies the Pythagorean relationship holds exactly: \[\|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2 = \|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2 + \|\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2\]



8.5.3 Wilks’ Theorem for Testing Reduced Models vs Full Models
The asymptotic distribution of the Likelihood Ratio Test statistic is given by the celebrated result from Wilks (1938). This theorem allows us to construct hypothesis tests without deriving the exact distribution of the likelihood ratio \(\Lambda\) for every specific model.

Theorem 8.4 (Wilks’ Theorem for Testing Reduced Models vs Full Models) Let \(\mathbf{Y} = (Y_1, \dots, Y_n)\) be an i.i.d. sample from a regular family of distributions with parameters \(\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{p_1}\). Let \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\) denote the true parameter value. Consider the test: \[H_0: \boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta_1\] where \(\Theta_0 \subset \Theta_1\) are smooth parameter spaces with dimensions \(p_0 < p_1\). Let \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}\) be the unrestricted MLE under \(H_1\), and \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\) be the restricted MLE under \(H_0\).
The likelihood ratio statistic is \(D = 2[\ell(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}) - \ell(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)})]\). Under \(H_0\), as \(n \to \infty\): \[D \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_{k}\] where \(k = p_1 - p_0\) is the difference in dimensionality between the models.


Proof. Geometric Proof via Quadratic Approximation
We rely on the geometric interpretation of the log-likelihood function as a squared distance metric induced by the Fisher Information matrix \(\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0)\).

Quadratic Approximation of the Log-Likelihood
We expand the log-likelihood \(\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) around the unrestricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}\). Since \(\nabla \ell(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}) = \mathbf{0}\), the linear term vanishes. Asymptotically, the Hessian converges to \(-n\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0)\): \[\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \approx \ell(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}) - \frac{n}{2} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta})^T \mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0) (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta})\] \[\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \approx \ell(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}) - \frac{n}{2} \|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2\]
This approximation replaces the likelihood surface with a parabola \(Q(\boldsymbol{\theta})\).
Approximation of the Restricted Estimator (Projection)
The true restricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\) maximizes the exact log-likelihood \(\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) over \(\Theta_0\). However, for the quadratic approximation, we define the Approximate Restricted MLE \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\) as the maximizer of the parabolic approximation \(Q(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) over \(\Theta_0\). This is equivalent to minimizing the distance to the unrestricted MLE: \[\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta_0} \|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2\]
Geometrically, \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\) is the orthogonal projection (as defined in Definition 8.6) of \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)}\) onto the subspace \(\Theta_0\) with respect to the metric \(\mathcal{I}\).
Crucial Justification: Why can we replace \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\) with \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\)? According to Le Cam’s Local Asymptotic Normality (LAN) theory (Le Cam, 1986), the likelihood surface becomes locally quadratic and the parameter space locally linear. Consequently, the difference between the true restricted maximum and the quadratic projection vanishes at a rate faster than the estimation error: \[\|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} - \tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\| = o_p(n^{-1/2})\] Thus, for the purpose of the asymptotic distribution, we use \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\).
Orthogonal Decomposition
Substituting the projection \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\) into the test statistic approximation: \[D \approx n \|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2\]
Using the Pythagorean Identity from Definition 8.6 with \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\) as the true parameter (which lies in \(\Theta_0\) under the null hypothesis): \[n \|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)}\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2 = n \|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2 - n \|\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2\]
Distributional Conclusion
Multiplying by \(n\), we identify the terms with Chi-square variables: \[\underbrace{D}_{\text{Statistic}} \approx \underbrace{n\|\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(1)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2}_{\sim \chi^2_{p_1}} - \underbrace{n\|\tilde{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{(0)} - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0\|_{\mathcal{I}}^2}_{\sim \chi^2_{p_0}}\]
By Cochran’s Theorem, the squared length of the projection of a standard normal vector onto the orthogonal complement of a subspace of dimension \(p_0\) is distributed as a Chi-square with degrees of freedom equal to the codimension: \[D \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_{p_1 - p_0}\]

References: Le Cam, L. (1986). Asymptotic Methods in Statistical Decision Theory. Springer-Verlag.


Example 8.5 LRT for Normal Mean with Unknown Variance
Let \(X_1, \dots, X_n \overset{i.i.d.}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) where both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are unknown. The parameter vector is \(\theta = (\mu, \sigma^2)\). We wish to test: \[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\]

Find the Maximum Likelihood Estimators: The log-likelihood function is \(\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu)^2\).

Unrestricted MLEs (\(\hat{\theta}\)): \(\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar{X})^2\). The maximized log-likelihood is: \[\ell(\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\hat{\sigma}^2) - \frac{n}{2}\]
Restricted MLEs (\(\hat{\theta}_0\)): Under \(H_0\), \(\hat{\mu}_0 = \mu_0, \quad \hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0)^2\). The restricted maximized log-likelihood is: \[\ell(\hat{\mu}_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\hat{\sigma}_0^2) - \frac{n}{2}\]

Define the Test Statistic \(D\): The test statistic \(D\) is defined as twice the difference between the unrestricted and restricted log-likelihoods: \[D = 2 \left[ \ell(\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^2) - \ell(\hat{\mu}_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2) \right]\] Substituting the expressions above, the constant terms cancel out: \[D = 2 \left[ -\frac{n}{2}\ln(\hat{\sigma}^2) - \left( -\frac{n}{2}\ln(\hat{\sigma}_0^2) \right) \right] = n \ln \left( \frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2} \right)\]
Relate to the t-statistic: Using the sum of squares decomposition \(\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 + (\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2\), we have: \[\frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2} = 1 + \frac{(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2}\] Substituting the t-statistic \(T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}}\), where \(S^2 = \frac{n}{n-1}\hat{\sigma}^2\), we find: \[\frac{(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2} = \frac{T^2}{n-1}\] Thus, \(D\) is a monotone increasing function of \(T^2\): \[D = n \ln \left( 1 + \frac{T^2}{n-1} \right)\] Rejecting \(H_0\) for large \(D\) is equivalent to rejecting for large \(|T|\).
Asymptotic Distribution (Wilks’ Theorem): By Wilks’ Theorem, \(D \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1\). For large \(n\), using the approximation \(\ln(1+x) \approx x\): \[D = n \ln \left( 1 + \frac{T^2}{n-1} \right) \approx n \left( \frac{T^2}{n-1} \right) \approx T^2\]
As \(n \to \infty\), \(T^2 \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1\), confirming the theorem.




8.5.4 Rao’s Score Theorem for Testing Restricted Models
The asymptotic distribution of the Score Test statistic relies on the behavior of the gradient of the log-likelihood (the Score vector) evaluated at the restricted Maximum Likelihood Estimator. This theorem provides a powerful method for hypothesis testing that only requires fitting the model under the null hypothesis.

Theorem 8.5 (Asymptotic Distribution of the Score Statistic) Let \(\mathbf{Y} = (Y_1, \dots, Y_n)\) be an i.i.d. sample from a regular family of distributions with parameters \(\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{p}\). Partition the parameter vector as \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^T, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1^T)^T\), where \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\) is the \(q\)-dimensional parameter of interest and \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\) is the \((p-q)\)-dimensional nuisance parameter. Consider the test: \[H_0: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^* \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 \neq \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*\]
Let \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1)\) be the restricted MLE under \(H_0\). Let \(\mathbf{U}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) be the total Score vector partitioned as \(\mathbf{U} = [\mathbf{U}_0^T, \mathbf{U}_1^T]^T\), and let \(\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) be the expected Fisher Information matrix with \(\mathcal{I}^{00}\) representing the top-left block of its inverse \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\).
The Score statistic is \(S = \mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)^T \mathcal{I}^{00}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) \mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)\). Under \(H_0\), as \(n \to \infty\): \[S \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\] where \(q\) is the dimension of the parameter of interest \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\).


Proof. Algebraic Proof via Orthogonal Projection of the Score
We rely on the first-order Taylor expansion to observe how estimating the nuisance parameter \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\) impacts the sampling distribution of the score vector for the parameter of interest \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\).

Partitioning the Information Matrix The expected Fisher Information matrix and its inverse are partitioned conformably with \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^T, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1^T)^T\): \[\mathcal{I} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathcal{I}_{00} & \mathcal{I}_{01} \\ \mathcal{I}_{10} & \mathcal{I}_{11} \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathcal{I}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathcal{I}^{00} & \mathcal{I}^{01} \\ \mathcal{I}^{10} & \mathcal{I}^{11} \end{pmatrix}\]
Estimation Error of the Nuisance Parameter Let \(\mathbf{U}_0\) and \(\mathbf{U}_1\) denote the unobservable true score vectors evaluated at the true parameters \((\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1)\). By definition, the restricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1\) forces the nuisance score to zero: \(\mathbf{U}_1(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1) = \mathbf{0}\).
Taking a first-order Taylor expansion of \(\mathbf{U}_1(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1)\) around the true parameter \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\): \[\mathbf{0} \approx \mathbf{U}_1 - \mathcal{I}_{11} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_1)\] Rearranging gives the asymptotic estimation error: \[(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_1) \approx \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \mathbf{U}_1\]
The Effective Score (Orthogonal Projection) Next, we expand the evaluated score of interest \(\mathbf{U}_0(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1)\) around the true parameter \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\): \[\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) \approx \mathbf{U}_0 - \mathcal{I}_{01} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_1)\]
Substituting the estimation error from Step 2: \[\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) \approx \mathbf{U}_0 - \mathcal{I}_{01} \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \mathbf{U}_1\]
This quantity is the Effective Score. Geometrically, it represents the residual of \(\mathbf{U}_0\) after projecting it orthogonally onto the nuisance score \(\mathbf{U}_1\).
Asymptotic Variance of the Effective Score We verify independence by checking the covariance between the effective score and the nuisance score: \[\text{Cov}(\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0), \mathbf{U}_1) \approx \text{Cov}(\mathbf{U}_0, \mathbf{U}_1) - \mathcal{I}_{01} \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \text{Var}(\mathbf{U}_1) = \mathcal{I}_{01} - \mathcal{I}_{01} \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \mathcal{I}_{11} = \mathbf{0}\]
Because they are jointly normal with zero covariance, they are independent. The variance of this orthogonalized score is the Schur complement of \(\mathcal{I}_{11}\): \[\text{Var}(\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)) = \mathcal{I}_{00} - \mathcal{I}_{01} \mathcal{I}_{11}^{-1} \mathcal{I}_{10}\] By the block matrix inversion theorem, this Schur complement is exactly the inverse of the top-left block of \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\): \[\text{Var}(\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)) = (\mathcal{I}^{00})^{-1}\]
Distributional Conclusion Because the evaluated score \(\mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)\) is asymptotically normal with mean zero and variance \((\mathcal{I}^{00})^{-1}\), standardizing it by its variance creates a quadratic form that follows a Chi-square distribution: \[S = \mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)^T \mathcal{I}^{00} \mathbf{U}_0(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\]



Example 8.6 Score Test for Normal Mean with Unknown Variance
Let \(X_1, \dots, X_n \overset{i.i.d.}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) where both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are unknown. The parameter vector is \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mu, \sigma^2)^T\). We wish to test: \[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\]

Find the Restricted MLEs: Under \(H_0\), we fix \(\mu = \mu_0\). Maximizing the restricted log-likelihood with respect to the nuisance parameter \(\sigma^2\) yields: \[\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0)^2\] The restricted parameter vector is \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 = (\mu_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2)^T\).
Evaluate the Full Score Vector: The full log-likelihood function for the normal sample is: \[\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu)^2\]
The total Score vector \(\mathbf{U}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) is the gradient with respect to both parameters: \[\mathbf{U}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{pmatrix} U_\mu(\mu, \sigma^2) \\ U_{\sigma^2}(\mu, \sigma^2) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu)}{\sigma^2} \\ -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu)^2 \end{pmatrix}\]
Now we evaluate this full vector at the restricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 = (\mu_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2)^T\): \[\mathbf{U}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \\ -\frac{n}{2\hat{\sigma}_0^2} + \frac{1}{2\hat{\sigma}_0^4}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0)^2 \end{pmatrix}\]
Because \(\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0)^2\), the second component perfectly cancels out. By definition, the score for the nuisance parameter evaluated at the restricted MLE is zero: \[\mathbf{U}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\]
Determine the Fisher Information: The expected Fisher Information matrix for a Normal distribution is diagonal because the mean and variance are orthogonal parameters: \[\mathcal{I}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n}{\sigma^2} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{n}{2\sigma^4} \end{pmatrix}\] Because the matrix is diagonal, the top-left block of the inverse \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\) is simply the reciprocal of the top-left block of \(\mathcal{I}\). Evaluated at \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0\): \[\mathcal{I}^{\mu\mu}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{n}\]
Compute the Score Statistic \(S\): Constructing the quadratic form for the parameter of interest: \[S = U_\mu(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)^T \mathcal{I}^{\mu\mu}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0) U_\mu(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0)\] \[S = \left( \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \right) \left( \frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{n} \right) \left( \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \right) = \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}\]
Asymptotic Distribution: By the Score Theorem, since we are testing \(q=1\) parameter restriction, the statistic follows a \(\chi^2_1\) distribution: \[S = \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}_0^2} \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1\] This is highly analogous to the squared t-statistic, but specifically uses the variance estimated under the null hypothesis constraint.




8.5.5 Wald’s Theorem for Testing Parameter Restrictions
The Wald Test evaluates the distance between the unrestricted Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) and the null hypothesis value, standardized by the curvature of the log-likelihood at the unrestricted estimate. Unlike the Score test, the Wald test requires estimating the full, unrestricted model.

Theorem 8.6 (Asymptotic Distribution of the Wald Statistic) Let \(\mathbf{Y} = (Y_1, \dots, Y_n)\) be an i.i.d. sample from a regular family of distributions with parameters \(\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{p}\). Partition the parameter vector as \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^T, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1^T)^T\), where \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\) is the \(q\)-dimensional parameter of interest and \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_1\) is the \((p-q)\)-dimensional nuisance parameter. Consider the test: \[H_0: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^* \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 \neq \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*\]
Let \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0^T, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_1^T)^T\) be the unrestricted MLE under \(H_1\). Let \(\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) be the expected Fisher Information matrix, and let \(\mathcal{I}^{00}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\) denote the top-left \(q \times q\) block of its inverse \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta})\).
The Wald statistic is defined as the quadratic form: \[W = (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*)^T [\mathcal{I}^{00}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})]^{-1} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*)\]
Under standard regularity conditions, if \(H_0\) is true, as \(n \to \infty\): \[W \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\] where \(q\) is the dimension of the parameter of interest \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0\).


Proof. Algebraic Proof via Asymptotic Normality of the MLE
The derivation of the Wald statistic relies directly on the asymptotic sampling distribution of the Maximum Likelihood Estimator.

Asymptotic Normality of the Full MLE By standard large-sample theory, the unrestricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\) is consistent and asymptotically normal. Its asymptotic variance-covariance matrix is the inverse of the Fisher Information matrix evaluated at the true parameter \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\): \[\sqrt{n}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} - \boldsymbol{\theta}) \xrightarrow{d} N(\mathbf{0}, [n^{-1}\mathcal{I}(\boldsymbol{\theta})]^{-1})\] Equivalently, for the unscaled estimator: \[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \overset{a}{\sim} N(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathcal{I}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\theta}))\]
Marginal Distribution of the Parameter of Interest We partition the inverse Fisher Information matrix \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\) conformably with \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^T, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1^T)^T\). The marginal asymptotic distribution of the sub-vector \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0\) depends only on the corresponding top-left block of the inverse matrix, \(\mathcal{I}^{00}\): \[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 \overset{a}{\sim} N(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0, \mathcal{I}^{00}(\boldsymbol{\theta}))\]
Under the null hypothesis \(H_0: \boldsymbol{\theta}_0 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*\), this becomes: \[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 \overset{a}{\sim} N(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \mathcal{I}^{00}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1))\]
Constructing the Quadratic Form
To create a test statistic, we measure the squared distance from the estimate \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0\) to the hypothesized value \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*\), standardized by its variance. If a random vector \(\mathbf{Z} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{\Sigma})\), then the quadratic form \((\mathbf{Z} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{Z} - \boldsymbol{\mu})\) follows a \(\chi^2\) distribution with degrees of freedom equal to the dimension of \(\mathbf{Z}\).
Applying this to our marginal distribution: \[(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*)^T [\mathcal{I}^{00}(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*, \boldsymbol{\theta}_1)]^{-1} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*) \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\]
Substituting the Consistent Estimator In practice, the true parameters in the variance matrix are unknown. Because the Fisher Information matrix is a continuous function of the parameters, and \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\) is a consistent estimator of \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\), we can invoke Slutsky’s Theorem. We substitute the unknown true information with the expected information evaluated at the unrestricted MLE, \(\mathcal{I}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})\), without changing the asymptotic distribution: \[W = (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*)^T [\mathcal{I}^{00}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})]^{-1} (\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_0 - \boldsymbol{\theta}_0^*) \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_q\]



Example 8.7 Wald Test for Normal Mean with Unknown Variance
Let \(X_1, \dots, X_n \overset{i.i.d.}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) where both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are unknown. The parameter vector is \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mu, \sigma^2)^T\). We wish to test: \[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\]

Find the Unrestricted MLEs: Maximizing the full log-likelihood with respect to both parameters yields the unrestricted estimators: \[\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar{X})^2\] Our unrestricted parameter estimate is \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^2)^T\).
Determine the Fisher Information at the MLE: The expected Fisher Information matrix for a Normal distribution is diagonal: \[\mathcal{I}(\mu, \sigma^2) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{n}{\sigma^2} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{n}{2\sigma^4} \end{pmatrix}\] Because the matrix is diagonal, the top-left block of the inverse \(\mathcal{I}^{-1}\) (the asymptotic variance of \(\hat{\mu}\)) is simply the reciprocal of the top-left block of \(\mathcal{I}\). Evaluated at the unrestricted MLE \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\): \[\mathcal{I}^{\mu\mu}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) = \frac{\hat{\sigma}^2}{n}\]
Compute the Wald Statistic \(W\): We construct the quadratic form using the parameter of interest (\(\mu\)), its null value (\(\mu_0\)), and its estimated variance: \[W = (\hat{\mu} - \mu_0)^T [\mathcal{I}^{\mu\mu}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})]^{-1} (\hat{\mu} - \mu_0)\] Substituting our values: \[W = (\bar{X} - \mu_0) \left( \frac{n}{\hat{\sigma}^2} \right) (\bar{X} - \mu_0) = \frac{n(\bar{X} - \mu_0)^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2}\]
Contrast with the Score Statistic: By Wald’s Theorem, this statistic follows a \(\chi^2_1\) distribution asymptotically. Note the critical difference from the Score test derived previously. The Wald statistic uses the unrestricted variance estimator \(\hat{\sigma}^2\), whereas the Score statistic used the restricted variance estimator \(\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (X_i - \mu_0)^2\). Because \(\hat{\sigma}_0^2 \ge \hat{\sigma}^2\), it guarantees that mathematically \(W \ge S\) for this model.




8.5.6 A Comparison of Finite-Sample Distributions of the Three Asymptotic Tests in OLS

8.5.6.1 Exact Finite-Sample Distributions via the F-Statistic
When testing linear restrictions in Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) under the assumption of normal errors, we do not need to rely on asymptotic approximations. The true, exact finite-sample test statistic follows an \(F\)-distribution.
Because the Wald (\(W\)), Likelihood Ratio (\(LR\)), and Score (\(S\)) statistics are strictly monotonic algebraic transformations of the exact finite-sample \(F\)-statistic, we can use the transformation of variables to derive their exact finite-sample Cumulative Distribution Functions (CDFs).
Let us consider testing a linear hypothesis involving \(q\) restrictions in an OLS model with \(k\) total parameters and \(n\) observations. Let \(F\) denote the exact finite-sample \(F\)-statistic, which follows an \(F_{q, n-k}\) distribution. Let \(G_{q, n-k}(x)\) denote the CDF of this \(F\)-distribution.
By isolating \(F\) in the algebraic definitions of \(W\), \(LR\), and \(S\), we derive their exact finite-sample CDFs:

Wald Statistic (\(W\)): \[W = \frac{nq}{n-k} F \implies F = \frac{n-k}{nq} W\] \[F_W(w) = P(W \le w) = G_{q, n-k}\left( \frac{n-k}{nq} w \right)\]
Likelihood Ratio Statistic (\(LR\)): \[LR = n \ln \left( 1 + \frac{q}{n-k} F \right) \implies F = \frac{n-k}{q} (e^{LR/n} - 1)\] \[F_{LR}(l) = P(LR \le l) = G_{q, n-k}\left( \frac{n-k}{q} (e^{l/n} - 1) \right)\]
Score Statistic (\(S\)): \[S = \frac{n q F}{n-k + q F} \implies F = \frac{S(n-k)}{q(n - S)}\] Because the Score statistic is bounded (\(S < n\)), its exact CDF for \(s < n\) is: \[F_S(s) = P(S \le s) = G_{q, n-k}\left( \frac{s(n-k)}{q(n - s)} \right)\] For \(s \ge n\), \(F_S(s) = 1\).



8.5.6.2 Visualizing Finite-Sample Calibration (Size Distortion)
To visualize how badly the asymptotic \(\chi^2_q\) approximation distorts the Type I error rates in finite samples, we can plot the true Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of the asymptotic p-values.
If a test is perfectly calibrated, the probability that its p-value is less than a nominal threshold \(\alpha\) should be exactly \(\alpha\), forming a perfect \(y = x\) diagonal line (the Uniform(0,1) CDF).
The event that the asymptotic p-value is less than \(\alpha\) is mathematically equivalent to the test statistic being greater than the \(\chi^2_q\) critical value (\(c_\alpha\)). Therefore, the true rejection probability \(P(p < \alpha)\) is exactly \(1 - F(c_\alpha)\), where \(F\) is the exact finite-sample CDF derived above.


Code
library(ggplot2)

# Define model parameters
n <- 15    # Small sample size
k <- 3     # Number of parameters in full model
q <- 1     # Number of restrictions

# Define the sequence of nominal alpha levels (focusing on the critical 0 to 0.10 range)
alpha_seq <- seq(0.001, 0.10, length.out = 500)

# The critical value from the claimed asymptotic Chi-square distribution
c_alpha <- qchisq(1 - alpha_seq, df = q)

# 1. Exact Rejection Probability for Wald: P(p < alpha) = 1 - F_W(c_alpha)
F_val_W <- ((n - k) / (n * q)) * c_alpha
cdf_W <- 1 - pf(F_val_W, df1 = q, df2 = n - k)

# 2. Exact Rejection Probability for Likelihood Ratio: P(p < alpha) = 1 - F_LR(c_alpha)
F_val_LR <- ((n - k) / q) * (exp(c_alpha / n) - 1)
cdf_LR <- 1 - pf(F_val_LR, df1 = q, df2 = n - k)

# 3. Exact Rejection Probability for Score: P(p < alpha) = 1 - F_S(c_alpha)
# Handling the upper bound where c_alpha >= n
F_val_S <- ifelse(c_alpha < n, (c_alpha * (n - k)) / (q * (n - c_alpha)), Inf)
cdf_S <- 1 - pf(F_val_S, df1 = q, df2 = n - k)

# Combine into a plotting data frame
plot_data <- data.frame(
  Alpha = rep(alpha_seq, 3),
  Rejection_Prob = c(cdf_W, cdf_LR, cdf_S),
  Test = factor(rep(c("1. Wald (Liberal)", "2. LR (Slightly Liberal)", "3. Score (Conservative)"), 
                    each = length(alpha_seq)))
)

# Plot the exact analytical CDFs
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = Alpha, y = Rejection_Prob, color = Test)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  # The perfectly calibrated Uniform(0,1) baseline: y = x
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("1. Wald (Liberal)" = "#E41A1C", 
                                "2. LR (Slightly Liberal)" = "#4DAF4A", 
                                "3. Score (Conservative)" = "#377EB8")) +
  labs(
    x = "Nominal Significance Level (\u03b1)",
    y = "True Rejection Rate: P(p-value < \u03b1)",
    title = "Exact Finite-Sample Calibration of Asymptotic Tests",
    subtitle = "Dashed line represents perfect Uniform(0,1) calibration"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.title = element_blank())








Figure 8.7: Exact Analytical CDFs of Asymptotic P-values (n=15, k=3, q=1)
